paragraphs.text:
%pyspark
# Setup and load saved models

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, length, when, lit, rand, udf
from pyspark.sql.types import ArrayType, StringType, FloatType
from pyspark.ml import PipelineModel
from pyspark.ml.evaluation import BinaryClassificationEvaluator
import time

# Re-ship jieba in case any executor needs it for prediction
spark.sparkContext.addPyFile("hdfs:///user/sic825/pyfiles/jieba.zip")

# Load both saved test models
print("Loading saved models from HDFS...")
model_en = PipelineModel.load("hdfs:///user/sic825/steam_reviews/models/english_lr_v1")
model_zh = PipelineModel.load("hdfs:///user/sic825/steam_reviews/models/schinese_lr_v1")
print("Loaded English and Schinese baseline models.")

# Reproduce the test sets for both languages
df_full = (spark.read.parquet("hdfs:///user/sic825/steam_reviews/parsed_full.parquet")
                .withColumn("recommended", (col("recommended") == "True").cast("boolean"))
                .withColumn("votes_helpful", col("votes_helpful").cast("long"))
                .withColumn("playtime_forever", col("playtime_forever").cast("double"))
                .withColumn("steam_purchase", (col("steam_purchase") == "True").cast("boolean"))
                .withColumn("label", col("recommended").cast("double")))

# English test set
df_en = (df_full.filter(col("language") == "english")
                 .filter(col("review").isNotNull() & col("recommended").isNotNull())
                 .filter((length(col("review")) >= 5) & (length(col("review")) <= 5000))
                 .withColumn("rand_split", rand(seed=42)))
test_en = df_en.filter(col("rand_split") >= 0.85).drop("rand_split").cache()

# Schinese test set with jieba tokenization (the saved model expects "tokens" column)
def jieba_tokenize(text):
    if text is None:
        return []
    import jieba
    tokens = [t.strip().lower() for t in jieba.cut(text) if t.strip()]
    tokens = [t for t in tokens if len(t) >= 1 and not t.isdigit()]
    return tokens

jieba_tokenize_udf = udf(jieba_tokenize, ArrayType(StringType()))

df_zh = (df_full.filter(col("language") == "schinese")
                 .filter(col("review").isNotNull() & col("recommended").isNotNull())
                 .filter((length(col("review")) >= 2) & (length(col("review")) <= 5000))
                 .withColumn("rand_split", rand(seed=42)))
test_zh = (df_zh.filter(col("rand_split") >= 0.85).drop("rand_split")
                 .withColumn("tokens", jieba_tokenize_udf(col("review")))
                 .cache())

print(f"\nEnglish test rows: {test_en.count():,}")
print(f"Schinese test rows: {test_zh.count():,}")

msg.data:
Loading saved models from HDFS...
Loaded English and Schinese baseline models.

English test rows: 1,342,812

paragraphs.text:
%pyspark
# Helper function for metrics

def compute_metrics(predictions, label_col="label", pred_col="prediction"):
    """Compute confusion matrix and per-class metrics for a binary classifier."""
    tp = predictions.filter((col(label_col) == 1.0) & (col(pred_col) == 1.0)).count()
    fp = predictions.filter((col(label_col) == 0.0) & (col(pred_col) == 1.0)).count()
    fn = predictions.filter((col(label_col) == 1.0) & (col(pred_col) == 0.0)).count()
    tn = predictions.filter((col(label_col) == 0.0) & (col(pred_col) == 0.0)).count()
    
    p_neg = tn / (tn + fn) if (tn + fn) > 0 else 0
    r_neg = tn / (tn + fp) if (tn + fp) > 0 else 0
    f1_neg = 2 * p_neg * r_neg / (p_neg + r_neg) if (p_neg + r_neg) > 0 else 0
    
    p_pos = tp / (tp + fp) if (tp + fp) > 0 else 0
    r_pos = tp / (tp + fn) if (tp + fn) > 0 else 0
    f1_pos = 2 * p_pos * r_pos / (p_pos + r_pos) if (p_pos + r_pos) > 0 else 0
    
    return {
        "tp": tp, "fp": fp, "fn": fn, "tn": tn,
        "neg_precision": p_neg, "neg_recall": r_neg, "neg_f1": f1_neg,
        "pos_precision": p_pos, "pos_recall": r_pos, "pos_f1": f1_pos,
        "macro_f1": (f1_neg + f1_pos) / 2
    }

print("Helper function defined.")

%pyspark
# Rerun baseline test evals

auc_eval = BinaryClassificationEvaluator(labelCol="label", rawPredictionCol="rawPrediction", metricName="areaUnderROC")

# English baseline
preds_en = model_en.transform(test_en).cache()
en_metrics = compute_metrics(preds_en)
en_auc = auc_eval.evaluate(preds_en)

# Schinese baseline
preds_zh = model_zh.transform(test_zh).cache()
zh_metrics = compute_metrics(preds_zh)
zh_auc = auc_eval.evaluate(preds_zh)

print("ENGLISH BASELINE (TF-IDF unigram + class weighting)")
print("=" * 60)
print(f"AUC:          {en_auc:.4f}")
print(f"Negative F1:  {en_metrics['neg_f1']:.4f}")
print(f"Positive F1:  {en_metrics['pos_f1']:.4f}")
print(f"Macro F1:     {en_metrics['macro_f1']:.4f}")

print("\nSCHINESE BASELINE (TF-IDF unigram + class weighting + jieba)")
print("=" * 60)
print(f"AUC:          {zh_auc:.4f}")
print(f"Negative F1:  {zh_metrics['neg_f1']:.4f}")
print(f"Positive F1:  {zh_metrics['pos_f1']:.4f}")
print(f"Macro F1:     {zh_metrics['macro_f1']:.4f}")

%pyspark
# Master comparison table

# Updated comparison table — adds English Word2Vec line
results = [
    # (language, model variant, AUC, neg_f1, macro_f1, training_data_size, training_time)
    ("English",  "Unweighted unigram",          0.9178, 0.5555, 0.7566, "6.3M",  "78s"),
    ("English",  "Weighted unigram",            0.9207, 0.6444, 0.7929, "6.3M",  "77s"),
    ("English",  "Weighted unigram+bigram",     0.9271, 0.6652, 0.8058, "6.3M",  "264s"),
    ("English",  "Weighted Word2Vec (sample)",  0.9021, 0.5166, 0.7028, "1.9M",  "1565s"),
    ("Schinese", "Unweighted unigram",          0.9177, 0.6651, 0.7917, "2.6M",  "26s"),
    ("Schinese", "Weighted unigram",            0.9168, 0.7545, 0.8347, "2.6M",  "28s"),
    ("Schinese", "Weighted unigram+bigram",     0.9257, 0.7717, 0.8473, "2.6M",  "136s"),
    ("Schinese", "Weighted Word2Vec (sample)",  0.9124, 0.7163, 0.8055, "0.78M", "867s"),
]

print("=" * 110)
print(f"{'Language':<10} {'Model variant':<32} {'AUC':<8} {'Neg F1':<8} {'Macro F1':<10} {'Train size':<10} {'Train time':<10}")
print("-" * 110)
for lang, variant, auc, nf1, mf1, size, t in results:
    print(f"{lang:<10} {variant:<32} {auc:<8.4f} {nf1:<8.4f} {mf1:<10.4f} {size:<10} {t:<10}")
print("=" * 110)

%pyspark
# Cross-language best models comparison

print("=" * 80)
print("BEST MODEL PER LANGUAGE — Direct comparison")
print("=" * 80)

best_models = [
    ("Metric",                    "English (bigram)",  "Schinese (bigram)",  "Δ (Schi - Eng)"),
    ("Train size",                "6,276,183",          "2,588,793",          "-58.7%"),
    ("% positive in train",       "89.05%",             "76.79%",             "-12.3pp"),
    ("ROC AUC",                   "0.9271",             "0.9257",             "-0.0014"),
    ("PR AUC",                    "0.9863",             "0.9696",             "-0.0167"),
    ("Negative class precision",  "0.5648",             "0.7140",             "+0.1492"),
    ("Negative class recall",     "0.8091",             "0.8395",             "+0.0304"),
    ("Negative class F1",         "0.6652",             "0.7717",             "+0.1065"),
    ("Positive class F1",         "0.9463",             "0.9229",             "-0.0234"),
    ("Macro F1",                  "0.8058",             "0.8473",             "+0.0415"),
]

for row in best_models:
    print(f"{row[0]:<28s} {row[1]:<22s} {row[2]:<22s} {row[3]}")

print("=" * 80)
print("\nKey finding: Schinese outperforms English on macro F1 (+4.2 points)")
print("driven by +10.7 point improvement on negative-class F1, despite less data.")
print("Hypothesis: lower class imbalance (77/23 vs 89/11) is the dominant factor.")

paragraphs.text:
%pyspark
# Effect of class weighting

print("=" * 80)
print("ABLATION — Effect of Class Weighting (validation set)")
print("=" * 80)
print(f"{'Variant':<25} {'English':<20} {'Schinese':<20}")
print("-" * 80)

print(f"\n{'Negative class recall':<25}")
print(f"  {'Unweighted':<23} {'0.4238':<20} {'0.5622':<20}")
print(f"  {'Weighted':<23} {'0.8022':<20} {'0.8367':<20}")
print(f"  {'Δ from weighting':<23} {'+0.3784':<20} {'+0.2745':<20}")

print(f"\n{'Negative class precision':<25}")
print(f"  {'Unweighted':<23} {'0.8060':<20} {'0.8142':<20}")
print(f"  {'Weighted':<23} {'0.5384':<20} {'0.6870':<20}")
print(f"  {'Δ from weighting':<23} {'-0.2676':<20} {'-0.1272':<20}")

print(f"\n{'Macro F1':<25}")
print(f"  {'Unweighted':<23} {'0.7566':<20} {'0.7917':<20}")
print(f"  {'Weighted':<23} {'0.7929':<20} {'0.8347':<20}")
print(f"  {'Δ from weighting':<23} {'+0.0363':<20} {'+0.0430':<20}")

print("\n" + "=" * 80)
print("Conclusion: Class weighting trades negative precision for recall in both languages.")
print("Net effect on Macro F1 is positive in both, slightly larger in Schinese.")

%pyspark
# Effect of n-grams

print("=" * 80)
print("ABLATION — Effect of Bigrams (validation set, all weighted)")
print("=" * 80)
print(f"{'Metric':<20} {'English Unigram':<18} {'English Bigram':<18} {'Δ':<10}")
print("-" * 80)
print(f"{'AUC':<20} {'0.9207':<18} {'0.9271':<18} {'+0.0064'}")
print(f"{'Negative F1':<20} {'0.6444':<18} {'0.6652':<18} {'+0.0208'}")
print(f"{'Macro F1':<20} {'0.7929':<18} {'0.8058':<18} {'+0.0129'}")
print()
print(f"{'Metric':<20} {'Schinese Unigram':<18} {'Schinese Bigram':<18} {'Δ':<10}")
print("-" * 80)
print(f"{'AUC':<20} {'0.9168':<18} {'0.9257':<18} {'+0.0089'}")
print(f"{'Negative F1':<20} {'0.7545':<18} {'0.7717':<18} {'+0.0172'}")
print(f"{'Macro F1':<20} {'0.8347':<18} {'0.8473':<18} {'+0.0126'}")

print("\n" + "=" * 80)
print("Bigrams improve performance in both languages similarly (~+1.3 macro F1).")
print("Suggests bigram value is consistent across language structures.")

%pyspark

print("=" * 90)
print("REPRESENTATION COMPARISON — TF-IDF vs Word2Vec across languages (validation)")
print("=" * 90)
print(f"{'Metric':<22} {'Variant':<22} {'English':<15} {'Schinese':<15}")
print("-" * 90)

print(f"{'AUC':<22} {'TF-IDF unigram':<22} {'0.9207':<15} {'0.9168':<15}")
print(f"{'':<22} {'TF-IDF bigram':<22} {'0.9271':<15} {'0.9257':<15}")
print(f"{'':<22} {'Word2Vec (sample)':<22} {'0.9021':<15} {'0.9124':<15}")

print()
print(f"{'Negative class F1':<22} {'TF-IDF unigram':<22} {'0.6444':<15} {'0.7545':<15}")
print(f"{'':<22} {'TF-IDF bigram':<22} {'0.6652':<15} {'0.7717':<15}")
print(f"{'':<22} {'Word2Vec (sample)':<22} {'0.5166':<15} {'0.7163':<15}")

print()
print(f"{'Macro F1':<22} {'TF-IDF unigram':<22} {'0.7929':<15} {'0.8347':<15}")
print(f"{'':<22} {'TF-IDF bigram':<22} {'0.8058':<15} {'0.8473':<15}")
print(f"{'':<22} {'Word2Vec (sample)':<22} {'0.7028':<15} {'0.8055':<15}")

print()
print(f"{'Training time':<22} {'TF-IDF unigram':<22} {'77s':<15} {'28s':<15}")
print(f"{'':<22} {'TF-IDF bigram':<22} {'264s':<15} {'136s':<15}")
print(f"{'':<22} {'Word2Vec (sample)':<22} {'1565s':<15} {'867s':<15}")

print("\n" + "=" * 90)
print("KEY FINDINGS:")
print("=" * 90)
print("""
1. TF-IDF outperforms Word2Vec on classification F1 in BOTH languages
   - English macro F1 drop: 0.79 -> 0.70 (-9 points)
   - Schinese macro F1 drop: 0.83 -> 0.81 (-3 points)

2. Word2Vec hurts English more than Schinese
   - English reviews are 4x longer on average (216 vs 52 chars)
   - Mean-pooling dilutes more strongly with more tokens to average
   - Suggests sparse representations are most valuable for long, morphologically
     rich text

3. Word2Vec's antonym problem visible in English: 'bad' is most similar to
   'ok' and 'good' (similarity ~0.7) because antonyms appear in similar contexts.
   This is precisely why bag-of-words wins for sentiment classification.

4. Despite lower classification F1, Word2Vec successfully learned semantic
   relationships (synonym discovery, slang variants, romanizations). The model
   is doing valuable work — just the wrong kind of work for this task.

5. TF-IDF is ~10-20x faster to train than Word2Vec. For this task,
   classical methods are both more accurate AND faster.
""")

==========================================================================================
REPRESENTATION COMPARISON — TF-IDF vs Word2Vec across languages (validation)
==========================================================================================
Metric                 Variant                English         Schinese       
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
AUC                    TF-IDF unigram         0.9207          0.9168         
                       TF-IDF bigram          0.9271          0.9257         
                       Word2Vec (sample)      0.9021          0.9124         

Negative class F1      TF-IDF unigram         0.6444          0.7545         
                       TF-IDF bigram          0.6652          0.7717         
                       Word2Vec (sample)      0.5166          0.7163         

Macro F1               TF-IDF unigram         0.7929          0.8347         
                       TF-IDF bigram          0.8058          0.8473         
                       Word2Vec (sample)      0.7028          0.8055         

Training time          TF-IDF unigram         77s             28s            
                       TF-IDF bigram          264s            136s           
                       Word2Vec (sample)      1565s           867s           

==========================================================================================
KEY FINDINGS:
==========================================================================================

1. TF-IDF outperforms Word2Vec on classification F1 in BOTH languages
   - English macro F1 drop: 0.79 -> 0.70 (-9 points)
   - Schinese macro F1 drop: 0.83 -> 0.81 (-3 points)

2. Word2Vec hurts English more than Schinese
   - English reviews are 4x longer on average (216 vs 52 chars)
   - Mean-pooling dilutes more strongly with more tokens to average
   - Suggests sparse representations are most valuable for long, morphologically
     rich text

3. Word2Vec's antonym problem visible in English: 'bad' is most similar to
   'ok' and 'good' (similarity ~0.7) because antonyms appear in similar contexts.
   This is precisely why bag-of-words wins for sentiment classification.

4. Despite lower classification F1, Word2Vec successfully learned semantic
   relationships (synonym discovery, slang variants, romanizations). The model
   is doing valuable work — just the wrong kind of work for this task.

5. TF-IDF is ~10-20x faster to train than Word2Vec. For this task,
   classical methods are both more accurate AND faster.

paragraphs.text:
%pyspark

print("""
================================================================================
WORD2VEC SEMANTIC DISCOVERY — what the embeddings learned
================================================================================

ENGLISH:

'good' is semantically similar to:
  great, nice, pretty, decent, awesome, cool
  (also 'bad' at similarity 0.68 — the antonym problem)

'buggy' is semantically similar to:
  glitchy, mess, unoptimized, laggy, broken, crashy, bugfest, jank
  (perfect cluster of "game has technical problems" vocabulary)

'refund' is semantically similar to:
  refunded, request, requested, requesting, denied, steams
  (the model learned the refund process language)

CHINESE:

不错 (not bad) is semantically similar to:
  很棒 (excellent), 比较满意 (satisfied), 挺耐玩 (replayable), 整体素质 (overall quality)

垃圾 (garbage) is semantically similar to:
  拉鸡, 辣鸡, lj, 乐色 (all variants/romanizations of "trash")
  Model learned slang and code-switched vocabulary

卡 (laggy) is semantically similar to:
  掉线 (disconnect), 卡屏 (screen freeze), 丢包 (packet loss), 卡掉 (lag out)
  Network/performance issues clustered, not other meanings of 卡

刺激 (exciting) is semantically similar to:
  紧张 (tense), 惊险 (thrilling), 真实 (realistic), 很爽 (satisfying)

================================================================================
TAKEAWAY: Word2Vec learned rich domain-specific vocabulary clusters in both
languages. The model captures synonyms, slang variants, romanizations, and
metaphorical associations. This is genuinely impressive emergent linguistic
knowledge — but it doesn't translate to better sentiment classification,
because mean-pooling dilutes the strong individual word signals that
sentiment classifiers need.
================================================================================
""")
